topic: The Genius And Failures of The Stock Market
title: How markets can create wealth and still punish crowds

lesson.learn:
The stock market is brilliant because it lets businesses raise money from many investors and gives people a way to own a share of productive companies. It is also flawed because prices can be driven by fear, hype, and short-term thinking. The market is not a perfect machine. It is a giant crowd constantly revising expectations.

That mix of intelligence and error is what makes the market powerful. Prices can help allocate capital efficiently, but they can also overshoot, crash, and reward speculation more than patience.
lesson.simpler: The stock market helps companies get money and lets people invest, but it can also get too excited or too scared.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Stock markets are useful because they move capital, but they are never perfectly rational.
lesson.deeperDive:
In theory, prices reflect information. In practice, markets are shaped by earnings, interest rates, sentiment, algorithms, and the stories investors tell each other. That is why booms and crashes keep appearing even when the underlying businesses do not change overnight.

The market works best when people treat it as a long-term ownership system rather than a quick gamble. Its genius is flexibility; its failure is that crowds can confuse momentum with truth.
lesson.funFact: The market can be smart and wrong at the same time.

people: Companies - raise capital by issuing stock
Regulators - try to keep markets fair
places: Company headquarters - where capital is put to work
years: 1602 - Amsterdam Exchange begins early share trading
concepts: Liquidity - how easily assets are traded
Valuation - the market's estimate of worth
